rulings.Case Title: IN RE: GRAMERCY TOWERS CONDOMINUM ASSOCAITON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Costs
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for January 22, 2026, line 1. RESPONDENT GRAMERCY TOWERS CONDOMINUM ASSOCIATION'S Motion For Costs. The motion for attorneys' fees and costs is denied. The court's tentative ruling in its entirety has been sent to counsel via email. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: OPTRICS INC ET AL VS. GRACE R. NEIBARON, ESQ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for January 22, 2026, line 2. DEFENDANT CLOUD9 DISCOVERY LLC'S Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint. The court denies leave here for two reasons. First, Cloud9 has provided an unsatisfactory explanation for bringing the cross-complaint at this late hour. Cloud9 offers no factual showing about when it learned that it had contractual indemnity claims. Its counsel's declaration states that Cloud9 "confirmed and produced" the agreement in July 2025 through prior counsel, but Cloud9 offers no evidence concerning whether prior counsel's failure to assert the claim was inadvertent or tactical. It appears that Cloud9 elected to go to mediation instead of asserting its cross-claims in September 2025 in the hopes of settling. Some or all of the other defendants have in fact settled with Optrics, but Cloud9 did not settle. As noted, the court presumes that Cloud9 had the ability to learn of the terms of its own contract from the very beginning of this litigation. Cloud9's motion was filed on December 1, 2025. The court concludes from these facts that Cloud9 acted not out of inadvertence or oversight but through an apparent tactical decision not to proceed with its cross-claim until it was the last defendant remaining in the case. Second, the court finds prejudice to the other parties. Given the current trial date of April 27, 2026, neither proposed cross-defendant will have sufficient time to conduct adequate discovery and file a dispositive motion for summary judgment/adjudication attacking the proposed claims. The proposed claims are not garden-variety indemnity claims but also involve whether Texas law applies and third-party beneficiary issues. The 5-year period to bring the action to trial is also fast approaching. The court's tentative ruling in its entirety has been sent to counsel via email. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. rulings.Case Title: ANDRE WILLIAMS ET AL VS. SAN FRANCISCO HOUSING AUTHORITY 1815 EGBERT AVE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for January 22, 2026, line 8. PLAINTIFFS ANDRE WILLIAMS, TIMOJENE WATSON'S Motion For Protective Order. Plaintiffs Andre Williams and Timojene Watson's motion for protective order is granted in part and denied in part. In general, sitting for deposition is an obligation of parties to a lawsuit. The court denies the request for an order prohibiting the deposition of Mr. Williams. Plaintiffs provide their own declarations that Mr. Williams has cognitive impairments, but they present no medical evidence. Moreover, Mr. Williams has signed declarations and discovery responses under penalty of perjury in this case. Signing a document under penalty of perjury represents to the court and other parties that the signer is capable of discerning and telling the truth; that is the same obligation a deponent has. The court grants the request for protective order insofar as it requests accommodations for both Mr. Williams and Ms. Watson. Defendants may conduct the depositions remotely or in person at a location designated by the deponent. Defendants shall accommodate both deponents with any requested breaks when no question is pending. The total deposition time shall not exceed seven hours and may be broken up into two to three hour blocks of time at deponents' request. Break time does not count towards the seven hours of total deposition time. Defendants shall endeavor to be as efficient as possible to minimize the burdens of deposition on plaintiffs. The court denies the request that Ms. Watson's deposition be limited to two hours, finding the accommodations noted above to be sufficient. Ms. Watson provides a declaration stating that she will agree to answer only limited questions (such as questions about what she observed when Mr. Williams sought accommodations). The court denies her request to limit the topics of deposition. In general, a deponent may only refuse to answer questions about attorney-client privileged material, absent some other court order about topics. Ms. Watson makes no showing that defendants intend to ask about topics that are not germane to the litigation. The parties shall meet and confer about mutually agreeable dates and arrangements for the depositions. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Case Title: SHIGEKO Y. BUTLER VS. AMERICAN HONDA MOTOR CO., INC., A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR LEAVE TO File An Amended Answer; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities And Declaration Of Joel Williams In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for January 22, 2026, line 9. DEFENDANT AMERICAN HONDA MOTOR CO. INC.'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO File An Amended Answer. The motion for leave to file an amended answer is granted. Defendant shall file the proposed amended answer attached as Exhibit A to the Williams Declaration forthwith and no later than January 30, 2026. Leave to amend a pleading is liberally granted and generally is refused only where inexcusable delay and prejudice exist. Here, there is unreasonable delay; plaintiff's complaint provides sufficient information about connection problems that defendant was on at least inquiry notice that plaintiff raised an infotainment system issue that might be covered by the release in Jimmy Banh v. American Honda Motor Co., Inc., Case No. 2:19-cv-5984. However, there is no cognizable prejudice. Plaintiff contends that there is necessary discovery about whether plaintiff is a member of the class or received notice of opt-out rights. The question of a preclusive effect of a prior judgment is generally a question of law, not a question of fact. The only fact question is whether plaintiff is a settlement class member, defined in the settlement agreement as "all current owners and lessees of Settlement Class Vehicles and former owners and lessees who file Claims but who have not otherwise already resolved and released their claims and who do not opt out of this Settlement." (Williams Decl., Ex. C.) This is not a question that requires discovery. The court also notes that, although plaintiff's papers say fact discovery is currently closed (Ardi Decl., para. 13), the order continuing the case for trial kept discovery open. (Order on Plaintiff's Ex Parte Application..., Oct. 17, 2025 ["All related dates concerning fact discovery ... are continued based on the newly set trial date ...."].) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address.=(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN MCGLYNN VS. EXTRA SPACE STORAGE INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for January 22, 2026, line 13. DEFENDANT EXTRA SPACE STORAGE INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION. Defendant Extra Space Storage Inc. moves to compel enforcement of an arbitration agreement purportedly entered by plaintiff John McGlynn, who has sued Extra Space. The court previously held a hearing and granted McGlynn's request for a continuance so that he could take the opportunity to obtain counsel. The court now grants the motion to compel arbitration. All claims against Extra Space are stayed in this court pending arbitration. The court's tentative ruling in its entirety has been sent to parties via email. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: KARENA APPLE FENG VS. FIFTH THIRD BANK ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: HEARING ON PLAINTIFF'S APPLICATION FOR A TEMPORARY RESTRAINING ORDER
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for January 22, 2026, line 14. HEARING ON PLAINTIFF'S APPLICATION FOR A TEMPORARY RESTRAINING ORDER. Plaintiff Karena Apple Feng's application for a temporary restraining order and OSC re preliminary injunction is granted in part and denied in part. Because Feng has not posted an undertaking, the court denies her request to return the 2014 Mercedes-Benz ML350 4MATIC (VIN: 4JGDA5HBOEA374039) that is the subject of this suit ("Car"). The court grants limited TRO relief as follows: it orders Fifth Third Bank and its co-defendant Day Break Metro Redemption Services not to sell the Car until the hearing on Feng's preliminary injunction motion. The court sets the OSC re preliminary injunction for March 10, 2026. The court's tentative ruling in its entirety has been sent to parties via email. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)
